channel.title: NYT > Climate and Environment
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Japan’s Cherry Blossom Database, 1,200 Years Old, Has a New Keeper
item.description: The remarkable catalog of dates is one of the longest-running records of climate change. Its creator died, setting off a search for a successor.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Yuichi Yamazaki/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Cherry blossoms at Rokkakudo Temple in Kyoto, Japan, recently.

item.title: Amit Forlit, Accused of Hacking Climate Activists, Appears in Court
item.description: Amit Forlit is accused of running a hacking operation that aimed to thwart climate litigation at the behest of a lobbying firm working for an oil giant.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Federal District Court in New York City. Amit Forlit has been indicted on charges of conspiracy to commit computer hacking, conspiracy to commit wire fraud and wire fraud.

item.title: Supreme Court Sides With Oil Companies in Louisiana Coastal Lawsuits
item.description: The companies had asked the justices to clear the way to move environmental lawsuits out of state courts, to friendlier federal venues.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Bryan Tarnowski for The New York Times
item.media:description: Wetlands along the Mississippi River as it nears the Gulf of Mexico in southeast Louisiana.

item.title: How Can I Protect My Car When the Weather Is Scorching?
item.description: Extreme heat is extremely hard on your vehicle, but a few simple precautions can help a lot.
item.dc:creator: Susan Shain
item.media:credit: Apu Gomes/Getty Images

item.title: Inside the Supreme Court’s Risky New Way of Doing Business
item.description: Secret memos obtained by The New York Times illuminate the origins of the court’s now-routine “shadow docket” rulings on presidential power.
item.dc:creator: Jodi Kantor and Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Key Excerpts From the Supreme Court’s Secret Memos
item.description: The New York Times obtained a trove of documents illuminating the inner workings of the court as it embraced a secretive track for making major decisions.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak and Jodi Kantor

item.title: Western States Need Water. San Diego Has Extra. Will They Make A Deal?
item.description: San Diego County is shopping a surplus of desalinated seawater to Western states that are facing increasingly urgent drought and short supplies.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: John Francis Peters for The New York Times
item.media:description: A view of San Diego and the Pacific Ocean from Cabrillo National Monument.

item.title: The Long-Term Plan to Scrub Carbon From the Sky
item.description: Microsoft is pulling back from efforts to remove carbon from the atmosphere. But the nascent industry’s proponents say they are thinking in decades, not years.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:credit: Jim Wilson/The New York Times
item.media:description: A carbon capture facility in Tracy, Calif., in 2023.

item.title: Court Rejects Trump Administration Climate Lawsuit Against Hawaii
item.description: In a setback for federal efforts to thwart climate litigation, the judge ruled that the suit, which tried to block the state from suing oil companies, was too speculative.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Phillip Jung for The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. District Court building in Honolulu. Senior Judge Helen Gillmor dismissed the Justice Department’s case with prejudice, meaning it cannot be refiled.

item.title: Senate Votes to Allow Mining Near Boundary Waters Wilderness
item.description: The move was a victory for a Chilean company that wants to build a copper and nickel mine, which environmentalists say could devastate fragile lakes and forests.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Tim Gruber for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness is one of the largest and most visited expanses of federally protected lakes and forests in the United States.

item.title: Carbon Removal Industry Reels as Microsoft Retreats
item.description: Once held up as a key solution to climate change, a field that aims to remove carbon from the atmosphere is struggling to catch on.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:credit: Ariana Gomez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Workers at a direct air capture facility under construction in Texas in 2024. Hundreds of start-ups developing carbon removal technologies have raised more than $5 billion in recent years.

item.title: Ford Says Doug Field, Who Led Company's E.V. Push, is Leaving
item.description: Doug Field, a former Tesla and Apple executive, had returned to Ford Motor in 2021 to help develop new electric models and software.
item.dc:creator: Neal E. Boudette
item.media:credit: Emily Elconin for The New York Times
item.media:description: Doug Field, a top electric vehicle executive, will leave Ford Motor next month.

item.title: Trump’s Go-To Justification for Contentious Decisions: National Security
item.description: The administration has invoked national security in a variety of matters, including the White House ballroom and offshore wind farms, drawing rebukes from some judges.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Construction of the White House ballroom project last month. An appeals court signaled that the Trump administration’s assertions about the national security implications of ballroom construction might be valid.

item.title: Trump Officials Pledge Swift Completion of Controversial Gas Pipeline
item.description: The $1 billion project will run for about 23 miles under Raritan Bay in New Jersey and New York Harbor. Environmental groups oppose it.
item.dc:creator: Hilary Howard
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Trump administration officials gathered on Tuesday in Brooklyn for the groundbreaking for a natural gas pipeline, including Chris Wright, left, the energy secretary; Lee Zeldin, third from left, the Environmental Protection Agency administrator; and Doug Burgum, second from right, the interior secretary.

item.title: The Treasury Secretary vs. Climate Science
item.description: In comments on Tuesday, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent dismissed the scientific consensus on the causes of climate change.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Oliver Contreras/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent speaking at the Institute of International Finance in Washington on Tuesday.

item.title: Bessent Questions the Cause of Climate Change and Its Economic Toll
item.description: The Treasury secretary said it is “difficult to deconstruct” the reasons for global warming, which he described as a belief of the “elite.”
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport and Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Evan Vucci/Reuters
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, right, said on Tuesday with the Danish academic Bjorn Lomborg that it was difficult to determine the causes of climate change.

item.title: Europe Is Desperate for More Energy. Can Norway Come to the Rescue?
item.description: The war in Iran has once again exposed Europe’s energy vulnerability. A friendly source at home would seem to be ideal. But it’s not that easy.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Carina Johansen/NTB Scanpix, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The Johan Sverdrup oil field in the North Sea west of Stavanger, off the southwestern coast of Norway.

item.title: Youths Who Sued Trump Over Orders to ‘Unleash’ Energy Try to Revive Case
item.description: Appealing the dismissal of their suit, they argued that executive orders to promote fossil fuels endangered their futures and violated their constitutional rights.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Amanda Lucier for The New York Times
item.media:description: Julia Olson, a lawyer for the plaintiffs, arriving at court on Monday.

item.title: Gray Whales Are Dying in San Francisco Bay
item.description: The animals might be entering the Bay in search of food as climate change disrupts traditional sources. They face huge risks from ships in the area.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: A dead gray whale washed up at Point Reyes Station, Calif., in 2019.

item.title: In the Desert, a ‘Cleaning Station’ for Ants
item.description: A unique “choreography” between two ant species suggests a distinctive partnership in which one provides a carwash service to the other.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil

item.title: The Northeast Hoped to Lead on Climate. Now It’s Rethinking.
item.description: Many blue states are rethinking ambitious strategies to cut emissions as they struggle with rising electricity costs and new hurdles for renewable energy.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Seth Wenig/Associated Press
item.media:description: Wind turbines intended for the South Fork offshore wind farm at State Pier in New London, Conn., in 2023.

item.title: Why Investing in Wind and Solar to Avoid Gas Shocks Hasn’t Added Up for Some
item.description: Renewable energy is cheaper to run than fossil fuels, especially with war choking oil supply. But it hasn’t turned out that way for some European countries, and the reason is complex.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown and Mira Rojanasakul
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: E.P.A. Says It Will End Biden’s Coal Ash Disposal Rules
item.description: Lee Zeldin, the E.P.A. administrator, said the changes would help U.S. “energy dominance.” Environmentalists said they threaten drinking water.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Wade Payne/Associated Press
item.media:description: The aftermath of a coal ash spill after a retention pond wall collapsed in Harriman, Tenn., in 2008.

item.title: Climate Denial Comes to Washington
item.description: A conference of climate change deniers, a warning about the world’s largest penguin species, record low snowpack in the West, plus more climate news.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.category: International Union for Conservation of Nature
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lee Zeldin, the E.P.A. administrator, at the International Conference on Climate Change, organized by the Heartland Institute, in Washington on Wednesday.

item.title: Volkswagen to End E.V. Production at Tennessee Plant
item.description: The German carmaker is the latest to scale back plans for electric vehicles in favor of gasoline models.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Sports Utility Vehicles and Light Trucks
item.media:credit: Bryan Derballa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Volkswagen said it would stop U.S. production of the ID.4, an electric sport utility vehicle.

item.title: Climate Change Denial Sees a Resurgence in Trump’s Washington
item.description: A conference near the White House drew hundreds of people who reject the scientific consensus on climate change. The mood was triumphant.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: William Happer, a Princeton professor emeritus of physics who has argued that global warming is good for humanity, during a presentation at the Heartland Institute’s International Climate Change Conference in Washington on Wednesday.

item.title: I.U.C.N. Red List Moves Emperor Penguins to “Endangered”
item.description: Populations are declining as climate change causes the sea ice the birds need for survival to retreat, according to researchers.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Nuwer
item.category: International Union for Conservation of Nature
item.media:credit: Imazins/Getty Images

item.title: Clean Energy Team Wins Salt River Project Election in Arizona
item.description: Proponents of renewable power will control the Phoenix area utility’s policymaking for the first time after they won an unusually contentious race that drew attention from national groups.
item.dc:creator: Reis Thebault
item.media:credit: Vanessa Abbitt/The Republic, via Imagn Images
item.media:description: The Salt River Project Agua Fria Generating Station in Glendale, Ariz., powers much of the Phoenix valley.

item.title: How War in the Middle East Paralyzed an Asian Food Giant
item.description: Vietnam, the world’s No. 2 rice exporter, cut production as power prices surged. Even with a temporary cease-fire in Iran, worries linger over the world’s food supply.
item.dc:creator: Damien Cave and Linh Pham
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A boat transporting newly harvested rice in My Thanh Ward, Dong Thap Province, Vietnam, in late March.

item.title: Heat Wipes Out Western Snowpack, Raising Fears of Summer Drought
item.description: A record-mild winter and blistering spring heat will mean higher risks of water shortages and wildfires in the months ahead.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance, Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Mira Rojanasakul
item.media:credit: Cheney Orr/Reuters
item.media:description: A puddle of water at the Keystone Ski Resort as temperatures reach into the mid 60s in Keystone, Colo., on March 21.

item.title: Why Electric Trucks Haven’t Taken Off in the U.S.
item.description: The vast majority of commercial trucks in the U.S. run on diesel, but that may be changing as fuel prices rise and Tesla ramps up production.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: An electric truck in the Port of Los Angeles in 2023.

item.title: How Tolls in the Strait of Hormuz Would Undercut International Law
item.description: Both the U.S. and Iran are now talking about imposing fees on ships using the vital passageway. That could fray a treaty that ended centuries of lawlessness on the high seas.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: A cargo ship in the Persian Gulf heading toward the Strait of Hormuz in recent weeks.

item.title: Suspect in Hacking of Climate Activists Is Extradited to New York
item.description: Prosecutors say Amit Forlit ran a global hacking operation on behalf of a Washington lobbying group that aimed to thwart environmental lawsuits against oil companies.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Brandon Bell/Getty Images
item.media:description: The clients of the lobbying firm that is alleged to have hired Amit Forlit included Exxon Mobil, according to a legal filing.
